original_abstract:
We present MS MARCO, a comprehensive dataset designed to advance research in MAchine Reading COmprehension (MRC) and question answering. Unlike many existing datasets that rely on synthetic questions or editorial content, MS MARCO consists of 1,010,916 unique, anonymized queries sampled from Bing’s search logs. These real-world questions are paired with 8,841,823 relevant passages extracted from web documents and human-synthesized natural language answers.

The dataset includes over 182,000 well-formed human written responses, and a significant portion of questions are designed to test a model's ability to identify unanswerable queries—a critical requirement for robust, real-world AI applications. We define three distinct tasks using this data: answer synthesis and extraction, well-formed answer generation, and passage ranking. By leveraging large-scale, naturally occurring user data rather than crowdsourced queries based on specific text spans, MS MARCO provides a more authentic distribution of human information-seeking behavior. We offer baseline evaluations using several state-of-the-art neural architectures and demonstrate that the complexity and scale of MS MARCO make it a rigorous benchmark for the next generation of intelligent agents.
humanized_abstract:
We introduce a new, large scale, open dataset, MS MARCO (Microsoft MARCO), for the purpose of advancing state of the art in the MRC (Machine Reading Comprehension) and question answering tasks. Almost all of the current datasets for these tasks either are built on top of artificially generated questions or on selective sets of questions that were chosen and sometimes even slightly modified by human editors. In contrast, MS MARCO uses 1,010,916 unique real questions that were chosen from anonymized search query history of Bing. Each of these real questions is paired with 8,841,823 relevant contexts, which are proportionally selected from web documents, as well as with human written natural language answers.

We release a dataset of over 182k human-written answers along with questions on which these answers are appropriate, and identify a subset of questions as “unk answerable” in order to train a model that can recognize when it cannot generate an answer. We derive three search benchmarks from this data: answer synthesis and extraction, well-formed answer generation, and passage ranking. Our data has several distinguishing features compared to other search benchmarks: - Our training set is derived from real human queries, rather than crowdsourced answers to specific passages, and thus reflects the natural distribution of information-seeking behaviors in a large population of users. - The dataset is significantly larger in scale than prior data, comprising nearly 10x more examples than the previous largest datasets for information retrieval and search, including TREC karpathoffersonly, TREC-QA, and ITRC. - Over 182k examples of well-formed, human-written answers are provided for model evaluation. We demonstrate how to do modeling on this data, derive baseline results on the derived benchmarks for several state-of-the-art neural network-based search models, and explain why we believe this data and the derived benchmarks will constitute a challenging next data and set of benchmarks for future search and AI research.

document.output:
We introduce a new, large scale, open dataset, MS MARCO (Microsoft MARCO), for the purpose of advancing state of the art in the MRC (Machine Reading Comprehension) and question answering tasks. Almost all of the current datasets for these tasks either are built on top of artificially generated questions or on selective sets of questions that were chosen and sometimes even slightly modified by human editors. In contrast, MS MARCO uses 1,010,916 unique real questions that were chosen from anonymized search query history of Bing. Each of these real questions is paired with 8,841,823 relevant contexts, which are proportionally selected from web documents, as well as with human written natural language answers.

We release a dataset of over 182k human-written answers along with questions on which these answers are appropriate, and identify a subset of questions as “unk answerable” in order to train a model that can recognize when it cannot generate an answer. We derive three search benchmarks from this data: answer synthesis and extraction, well-formed answer generation, and passage ranking. Our data has several distinguishing features compared to other search benchmarks: - Our training set is derived from real human queries, rather than crowdsourced answers to specific passages, and thus reflects the natural distribution of information-seeking behaviors in a large population of users. - The dataset is significantly larger in scale than prior data, comprising nearly 10x more examples than the previous largest datasets for information retrieval and search, including TREC karpathoffersonly, TREC-QA, and ITRC. - Over 182k examples of well-formed, human-written answers are provided for model evaluation. We demonstrate how to do modeling on this data, derive baseline results on the derived benchmarks for several state-of-the-art neural network-based search models, and explain why we believe this data and the derived benchmarks will constitute a challenging next data and set of benchmarks for future search and AI research.
document.input:
We present MS MARCO, a comprehensive dataset designed to advance research in MAchine Reading COmprehension (MRC) and question answering. Unlike many existing datasets that rely on synthetic questions or editorial content, MS MARCO consists of 1,010,916 unique, anonymized queries sampled from Bing’s search logs. These real-world questions are paired with 8,841,823 relevant passages extracted from web documents and human-synthesized natural language answers.

The dataset includes over 182,000 well-formed human written responses, and a significant portion of questions are designed to test a model's ability to identify unanswerable queries—a critical requirement for robust, real-world AI applications. We define three distinct tasks using this data: answer synthesis and extraction, well-formed answer generation, and passage ranking. By leveraging large-scale, naturally occurring user data rather than crowdsourced queries based on specific text spans, MS MARCO provides a more authentic distribution of human information-seeking behavior. We offer baseline evaluations using several state-of-the-art neural architectures and demonstrate that the complexity and scale of MS MARCO make it a rigorous benchmark for the next generation of intelligent agents.
